node.lean:
theorem frobenius_char_p
    {R : Type*} [CommRing R] {p : ℕ} (hp : Nat.Prime p) [CharP R p] (x y : R) :

node.natural_language_proof:
Proof: expand `(x + y)^p` by the binomial theorem as a finite sum over `k = 0,1,...,p`:
`(x + y)^p = sum_{k=0}^p (p choose k) x^k y^(p-k)`. For `0 < k < p`, the binomial coefficient
`(p choose k)` is divisible by `p` (since `p` is prime). In characteristic `p` this means
`(p choose k) = 0` in `R`, so all the middle terms vanish. Only the `k = 0` and `k = p` terms
remain, giving `(x + y)^p = y^p + x^p = x^p + y^p`.

node.lean:
theorem frobenius_char_p
    {R : Type*} [CommRing R] {p : ℕ} (hp : Nat.Prime p) [CharP R p] (x y : R) :
    (x + y) ^ p = x ^ p + y ^ p
node.natural_language_proof:
Proof: expand `(x + y)^p` by the binomial theorem as a finite sum over `k = 0,1,...,p`:
`(x + y)^p = sum_{k=0}^p (p choose k) x^k y^(p-k)`. For `0 < k < p`, the binomial coefficient
`(p choose k)` is divisible by `p` (since `p` is prime). In characteristic `p` this means
`(p choose k) = 0` in `R`, so all the middle terms vanish. Only the `k = 0` and `k = p` terms
remain, giving `(x + y)^p = y^p + x^p = x^p + y^p`.
